text: In 2017, Apple introduced a Do Not Disturb mode that silenced notifications during designated hours. Google followed with Digital Wellbeing tools in Android. Former Google design ethicist Tristan Harris observed that these features were structurally inadequate: they asked users to opt out of disruption rather than requiring platforms to justify each interruption. Harris argued for a 'notification audit' principle — that every notification a product sends should pass a minimum threshold of genuine user benefit before being permitted. Under this standard, most social platform notifications, engineered primarily to drive re-engagement rather than deliver value, would fail.
question: What was Tristan Harris's core critique of Apple's Do Not Disturb and similar opt-out tools?

options.text: They were too difficult for average users to configure correctly
They blocked beneficial notifications alongside manipulative ones
They required users to opt out rather than requiring platforms to justify each interruption
They did not address the underlying variable reward mechanisms in apps
